paragraphs: His work is, in some sense, a collection of anecdotes. It's quite fascinating to read and consult. Combining amusement and information, Suetonius's "Lives of the Caesars" was highly regarded. By the time the printing press was invented in the early 16th century, as many as eighteen editions had already been published, with nearly a hundred more added since then. His translation, however, was quite diffuse and retained most of the inaccuracies found in Clarke's work, upon which it was based. Consequently, considerable care had been taken to correct these errors, with the aim of producing a literal and faithful version. Lives of the most interesting characters are filled with anecdotes and fascinating stories that shed light on the lives of scholars and writers during the time period in question.

Julius Caesar, the divine, lost his father at the age of 16. The following year, nominated for the high-priest office of Jupiter, he rejected Cossutia, who was wealthy despite her family's equestrian status. He had been contracted to her when just a boy. He then married Cornelia, the daughter of Cinna, who was four-time consul, and had by her, shortly afterwards, a daughter named Julia. After changing his place of concealment nearly every night, despite suffering from a quartan ague, he eventually secured his release by bribing the officers who had tracked his footsteps. He then obtained a pardon through the intercession of the vestal virgins and his close relatives, Mamercus Aemilius and Aurelius Cotta.

We are assured that when Sylla finally yielded to the entreaties of his own best friends, persons of distinguished rank, he exclaimed: "Your suit is granted, and you may take him among you. However, know that this man, for whose safety you are so anxious, will one day be the ruin of the party of nobles in defense of which you are allied with me; for in this Caesar, you will find many a Marius." " II Despite his reservations about the leader's abilities, Mark Antony found the timing unfavorable for the project and abandoned plans to join forces with Lepidus, despite receiving lucrative offers. He soon preferred a charge of extortion against Cornelius Dolabella, a man of consular dignity, who had obtained the honor of a triumph.

On the acquittal of the accused, he resolved to retire to Rhodes, where he could avoid the public odium he had incurred and pursue his studies with leisure and tranquility, under the guidance of Apollonius, the son of Molon, who was then the most celebrated master of rhetoric. He was taken by pirates while on his voyage to the island of Pharmacusa during the winter season. They detained him for nearly 40 days, fueling his anger. His only companions were a physician and two chamberlains. At that time Mithridates was ravaging neighboring districts. Upon Caesar's arrival at Rhodes, he decided not to appear idle while danger threatened Rome's allies. He crossed into Asia and gathered some auxiliary forces. With these forces, he drove the king's governor out of the province and secured the allegiance of the cities that were wavering and ready to revolt. Having been elected military tribune, he was the first to receive honors from the people upon his return to Rome. He then zealously assisted those taking measures to restore the tribunate's authority, which had been significantly weakened during Sulla's usurpation.

He likewise, at the suggestion of Plotius, proposed an act to the people that led to Lucius Cinna, his wife's brother, and others with him being recalled. These individuals had been supporters of Lepidus during the civil unrest and, following the consul's death, had fled to Sertorius. VI For the Roman kings, starting with Marcus Marcius (also known as Ancus Marcius), we can trace their lineage back to his family. Similarly, for the Julian clan, tracing it back to Venus' descendants, we find that our family line is a branch of theirs. We therefore unite in our descent the sacred majesty of kings, the chiefest among men, and the divine majesty of gods, to whom kings themselves are subject. He married Pompeia, the daughter of Quintus Pompeius, and granddaughter of Lucius Sulla. However, he later divorced her on suspicion that she had been involved with Publius Clodius. During the celebration of a religious solemnity, Clodius had gained access to the report, disguised as a woman, and the senate subsequently instituted an inquiry into the profanation of sacred rites.

He therefore immediately sued for his discharge, with the view of seizing the first opportunity that presented itself in the city to enter upon a more exalted career. Leaving the province before its usual term expired, he went to the Latin colonies, which were then actively promoting Rome's freedom; and he would have encouraged them to make a bold move, but the consuls prevented any unrest by keeping the legions meant for service in Cilicia on hold for some time. He made an even greater effort within the city.

For only a few days before he entered upon the aedileship, he was suspected of having conspired with Marcus Crassus, a man of consular rank. Publius Sylla and Lucius Autronius, who later became consuls, were subsequently convicted of bribery. It was agreed upon that Piso would lead a revolt in the provinces, while the other would try to incite an insurrection at Rome. They planned to use the Lambrani and the tribes beyond the Po as their tools. To diminish their influence, he restored the trophies erected in honour of Caius Marius, who had won victories over Jugurtha, the Cimbri, and the Teutoni. These trophies had been demolished by Sylla. When sitting in judgment on murderers, he treated those who received money from the treasury for bringing in Roman citizens' heads as assassins. This was despite them being exempt under the Cornelian laws.

He calculated the vast amount of debt he had incurred and, upon leaving for the assembly of the people in the morning, told his mother as she kissed him goodbye, "I will not return home until I am elected pope." He left two of his most powerful competitors far behind, who were both older and held higher ranks. As a result, he received more votes from their own tribes combined than they did from all the other tribes put together. After he was chosen praetor, a conspiracy by Catiline was uncovered; and while every other member of the senate voted to impose capital punishment on the accomplices in that crime, he alone suggested that the delinquents be placed under safe custody among the towns of Italy, with their property confiscated. He persisted, however, in obstructing the measure, until a body of the Roman knights, who stood under arms as a guard, threatened him with instant death, if he refused to relent.

He was finally deterred by the violence and gave up, absenting himself from the senate house for the rest of the year. He subsequently proved himself to be a steadfast supporter of Caecilius Metellus, tribune of the people, who, despite the opposition from his fellow tribunes, had introduced several laws with a strong and violent tone. He likewise pacified the mob, which two days later flocked about him, and in a riotous manner offered their assistance in the vindication of his honor.

The senators, meeting in haste due to the commotion, expressed their gratitude to him through several prominent members of the house. They then summoned him, praising his behavior highly, and subsequently rescinded their previous decision, restoring him to his position. He therefore obliged Vettius to give pledges for his behavior, seized his goods, and after heavily fining him, saw him almost torn in pieces before the rostra, and then threw him into prison. He also sent Novius, the quaestor, to prison for having presumed to take an information against a magistrate of superior authority. He departed contrary to law and custom, taking his gear before the usual preparations were made.

The day of the election, however, was already fixed by proclamation, so he could not legally be admitted as a candidate, unless he entered the city as a private person. Caesar, incensed at this indignity, endeavoured by the most assiduous and flattering attentions to win over Cneius Pompey, who was dissatisfied with the Senate's refusal to confirm his actions following his victories over Mithridates. From that time, therefore, Caesar had sole management of public affairs. Some wags, when signing instruments as witnesses, would only include "of Julius" followed by "and Caesar." This practice resulted in the same person being listed twice, once under their name and again under their surname.

At last, he effectively intimidated all those of the opposite party. Vettius was prevailed upon by great rewards to declare that he had been solicited by certain persons to assassinate Pompey. When brought before the rostra to name those who had been concerted between them, after naming one or two to no purpose, Caesar became suspicious of subornation. Despairing of success in this rash stratagem, Caesar is supposed to have taken off his informer with poison. After this new alliance, he began to seek Pompey's opinion first in any debates in the senate, a distinction he had previously reserved for Marcus Crassus. It was the usual practice for the consul to observe throughout the year the method of consulting the senate that he had adopted on the calends of January.

He initially received only Cisalpine-Gaul, with Illyricum added, as a result of Vatinius' proposal to the people. Later, he obtained Gallia-Comata from the senate, as they were concerned that if they denied it to him, the province would be granted by the people instead. With his success, he couldn't help but boast a few days later in a full senate house, stating that despite his enemies and their great frustration, he had achieved everything he wanted, and from then on, he would make them humbly submit to his will. When the term of his consulship had expired, Caius Memmius and Lucius Domitius, the praetors, moved in the senate to discuss the transactions of the past year. He offered to refer himself to the house, but they declined the business. After three days of futile argumentation, he left for his province.

His quaestor was immediately charged with several misdemeanors, aimed at implicating Caesar. An accusation was soon made against him by Lucius Antistius, tribune of the people. However, he successfully appealed to his fellow tribunes, which led to the suspension of the prosecution while he was serving the state. To secure himself for the time to come, he was particularly careful to win the good-will of the magistrates at annual elections. He did not assist any candidates with his interest and refused to support anyone who would not positively undertake to defend him in his absence. For this purpose, he required an oath from some of them and even a written obligation.

He now acted presumptuously, having added to his own private expense more legions than those he had received from the republic. Among these was one levied in Transalpine Gaul, known as Alauda, which he trained and armed in the Roman style, and later granted the freedom of the city to it. He declined no occasion of war, however unjust and dangerous. Instead, he attacked both the allies of Rome and the barbarous nations that were its enemies, without provocation. As a result, the senate passed a decree to send commissioners to examine the condition of Gaul. Some members even suggested that he should be handed over to the enemy. During nine years that he governed the province, his achievements were as follows: He reduced all Gaul, bounded by the Pyrenean forest, the Alps, Mount Gebenna, and the two rivers, the Rhine and the Rhone, which spanned approximately three thousand miles in circumference. He transformed this territory into a province, with the exception of nations allied to the republic and those that had earned his favor. To consolidate this new acquisition, he imposed an annual tribute of forty million sesterces.

He lost his mother, whose death was followed by that of his daughter, and not long after, his granddaughter. Meanwhile, the republic was in turmoil over Publius Clodius' murder, and the senate voted to limit the consulship to a single holder for the upcoming year. He successfully persuaded the tribunes of the people, who were planning to support him in nominating Pompey, to propose a bill that would allow him to run for his second consulship even if he was absent, thereby avoiding the need to leave his province prematurely and ensuring he could complete the war before its conclusion. He had agreed with victuallers of all denominations for his feast, but the more he raised their expectations on this occasion, he made further preparations in private houses.

He issued an order: if the most celebrated gladiators ever incurred the displeasure of the public during a fight, they were to be seized by force and set aside for future use. Young gladiators he trained up, not in a school, but by masters of defence, in the houses of Roman knights and senators who were skilled in arms. They earnestly requested him, as his letters show, to take on the discipline of those novitiates and to give them word during their exercises. Caesar's claim to be a candidate for consul at the next election should not be considered, since Caesar was absent and Pompey later revoked this privilege through a decree passed by the people. Pompey, in his law relating to the choice of chief magistrates, had overlooked Caesar, in the article declaring all absent individuals incapable of being candidates for any office. However, soon after the law was inscribed on brass and deposited in the treasury, Pompey corrected his oversight.

Marcellus, dissatisfied with depriving Caesar of his provinces and the privilege intended for him by Pompey, also moved the senate to revoke the freedom of the city from those colonists whom he had settled at New Como. This decision was made because it had been granted to them with ambitious intentions, contrary to the laws. (20) XXIX Gnaeus Pompey often said that he aimed to create chaos, as he was unable to finish the projects he started due to his limited financial resources. Upon his return, he faced immense pressure from the public who had high expectations of him. When intelligence was received that the interposition of the tribunes in his favour had been utterly rejected, and they themselves had fled from the city, he immediately sent forward some cohorts, but privately, to prevent any suspicion of his design. To maintain appearances, he attended a public spectacle, examined the model of a fencing school he proposed to build, and then sat down to dinner with a large party of his friends.

As night fell, mules were led from a nearby mill to his carriage, allowing him to depart with as much secrecy as possible, accompanied by a modest entourage. As he approached the banks of the Rubicon, which marked the boundary of his province [58], Julius Caesar halted for a moment, weighing the significance of the decision before him. Turning to his troops, he said: "We can still retreat, but if we cross this bridge, our only option is to fight it out." " XXXII He was thus hesitant when a strange incident happened next.

He then returned to Rome, where he crossed the sea to Macedonia. There, he besieged Pompey for nearly four months, using a series of fortifications that stretched for miles. Eventually, he emerged victorious in the Battle of Pharsalus. He succeeded, however, in his enterprise, and placed the kingdom of Egypt in the hands of Cleopatra and her younger brother. He was afraid that if he made it a province, it would become the center of revolt under an ambitious prefect. Caesar, however, defeated his enemy just five days after entering his country, and four hours after coming into view of him, in a single decisive battle.

He then defeated Scipio and Juba, who were rallying the remnants of Pompey's party in Africa, and Pompey's sons in Spain. During the entire course of the civil war, he never once suffered any defeat. However, his lieutenants faced setbacks: Caius Curio fell in Africa, Caius Antonius was captured in Illyricum, and Publius Dolabella lost a fleet in the same region. In every encounter with the enemy where he commanded, he came off with complete success. The issue was never doubtful, except on two occasions: once at Dyrrachium, when he was forced to yield ground and Pompey failed to pursue his advantage, prompting him to say that "Pompey did not know how to conquer." The other instance occurred in his last battle in Spain, where, despite being desperate about the outcome, he even considered taking his own life.

For the victories he won in various wars, he triumphed five times. After Scipio's defeat, he won four more times within a single month, with each triumph following the previous one just a few days later. He also won once after the conquest of Pompey's sons. As he proceeded along the street called Velabrum on the day of the Gallic triumph, he narrowly escaped a fall from his chariot when the axle-tree broke. He then ascended the Capitol by torchlight, accompanied by forty elephants, each carrying torches on either side. He also provided public entertainment and distributed meat. Following his Spanish victory, he hosted two public dinners.

Considering the first gift he had given was deemed too sparse and unsuitable for his generous nature, he gave a second gift five days later, which was more abundant. He then turned his attention to regulating the commonwealth. He corrected the calendar, which had been excessively confused for some time due to the pontiffs' excessive freedom in intercalating dates. Crimes were punished with greater severity. The wealthy, who were more likely to commit crimes, faced less severe punishment since they could avoid exile by forfeiting only half their property. In contrast, murderers, as observed by Cicero, lost their entire estates, while other offenders lost only half of theirs.

He expelled from the senate those members who were convicted of bribery. He also dissolved the marriage of a man of Pretorian rank, who had married a woman just two days after she had divorced her previous husband, despite there being no suspicion that they had engaged in any illicit activities. He was described as tall, with a fair complexion, rounded limbs, and a full face. His eyes were black and piercing. He enjoyed excellent health, but towards the end of his life, he suffered from occasional fainting spells and disturbed sleep. He was similarly afflicted twice by the falling sickness while on active duty.

He therefore brought forward the hair from the crown of his head; and among all the honors conferred upon him by the senate and people, there was none that he either accepted or used with greater pleasure than the right to wear a laurel wreath constantly. It is said that he was particular about his dress. He first lived in a small house in the Suburra, but after his advancement to the pontificate, he occupied a palace belonging to the state in the Via Sacra.

Many writers say that he liked his residence to be elegant, and his entertainments sumptuous. He took down a villa near the grove of Aricia, which he had built from the foundation but did not finish at first because it did not exactly suit his taste, despite having slender means and being in debt. He was extremely meticulous in managing his domestic affairs, both minor and significant. On one occasion, he imprisoned a baker for serving him better bread than that offered to his guests. He also put to death a freedman who had indulged in an illicit relationship with the lady of a Roman knight, despite no complaint having been made to him about the matter. He subsequently funded the cost of the civil wars and his triumphs and public spectacles through the most blatant robbery and desecration.

After his prosecution of Dolabella, he was undoubtedly considered one of the most distinguished advocates. Of all the orators who, throughout their lives, devoted themselves solely to this pursuit, it is hard to find anyone more accomplished than him. In his delivery, he was known for a sharp voice and an animated, yet dignified, demeanor.

He composed the first two books in his passage over the Alps, as he was returning to the army after making his circuit in Hither-Gaul. The second work was written about the time of the battle of Munda. The last book was written during the 24 days he spent on his journey from Rome to Farther-Spain. Some things likewise pass under his name, said to have been written by him when he was a boy or very young man. These include The Encomium of Hercules, a tragedy titled Oedipus, and a collection of Apophthegms. Augustus forbade their publication in a brief letter to Pompeius Macer, who was responsible for arranging his libraries. (38) LVII He crossed the sea from Brundisium and Dyrrachium in winter, navigating through enemy fleets. The troops, under orders to join him, moved slowly despite repeated messages urging them to hasten. However, their efforts were futile. He decided to proceed privately and alone aboard a small vessel at night, with his head muffled, and waited until the wind was strong enough to propel them forward.

When a victim, which he was about to offer in sacrifice, escaped, he did not therefore defer his expedition against Scipio and Juba. He fought pitched battles and made sudden attacks when an opportunity arose; often at the end of a march, and sometimes during violent storms, when it seemed unlikely he would stir. A statue of this horse was later erected by Caesar's order before the temple of Venus Genitrix.

He often rallied his troops when they were giving way by his personal efforts. He would stop those who fled, keep others in their ranks, and seize them by the throat to turn them towards the enemy. Despite being outnumbered, an eagle-bearer was so terrified that he made a thrust at him with his spear-head. On another occasion, he left the standard in the hand of one of his men. After the Battle of Pharsalia, having sent his troops ahead into Asia, Julius Caesar met Lucius Cassius, one of his opponents, with ten ships of war. As Caesar was passing through the Hellespont in a ferryboat, they encountered each other. Instead of attempting to flee, Caesar approached Cassius' ship and asked him to surrender. Cassius, humbly submitting to Caesar's authority, yielded. At Alexandria, a sudden sally by the enemy forced him to take refuge in a boat. As others rushed in beside him, he leaped into the sea and swam to the next ship, which lay about 200 paces away. Holding his left hand out of the water to prevent it from getting wet, he clutched papers he held in it. With his teeth, he pulled his general's cloak after him, fearing it would fall into enemy hands.

When his troops were disheartened by reports of the enemy's great force, he rallied their spirits. He did this by acknowledging the truth of the information and emphasizing its significance, rather than downplaying it. Accordingly, when his troops were in great alarm at the expected arrival of King Juba, he called them together and said, "I have to inform you that in a very few days the king will be here. He will bring ten legions, thirty thousand horsemen, one hundred thousand light-armed infantry, and three hundred war elephants." Let none of you presume to make further enquiry or indulge in conjectures. Take my word for what I tell you, which I have from undoubted intelligence. If you do not believe me, I shall put you aboard an old, crazy vessel and leave you exposed to the mercy of the winds, to be transported to some other country. " LXVII After a great battle ending in victory, he would grant them a relaxation from all kinds of duty, and leave them to revel in pleasure. Being accustomed to boasting, "That his soldiers fought no worse for being well-oiled." "In his speeches, he never referred to them as 'Soldiers,' but instead used the kinder phrase 'Fellow-soldiers.' He maintained their order with great precision, ensuring that their arms were adorned with silver and gold, not just for display, but to instill a sense of resolve in them, making them more determined to fight bravely."

None. They endured hardship and other difficulties, not only when they were besieged themselves, but also when they besieged others. When Pompey was blockaded in the vicinity of Dyrrachium, he saw a type of bread made from an herb that the rebels lived on. He remarked, "I am dealing with wild beasts," and ordered it to be removed immediately. This was because if his troops were to see such bread, their spirits might be broken by witnessing the enemy's endurance and determination. With what bravery they fought, one instance affords sufficient proof. This is evident in the fact that after an unsuccessful engagement at Dyrrachium, they were called upon for punishment. However, their general found it more necessary to comfort them than to inflict punishment.

They defeated immense armies of the enemy with ease in various battles and regions. A single cohort of the sixth legion held out a fort for several hours against four legions under Pompey. Many of its soldiers were wounded by the large number of arrows fired at them, with over 130,000 arrows found within the fort's walls. Scavva, after losing an eye, had it struck out, was run through the thigh and shoulder, and had his shield pierced in 120 places. He stubbornly defended the gate of a fortress, to which he was entrusted.

They never once rebelled during all the ten years of the Gallic War, but were sometimes disobedient in the course of the civil war. They always returned quickly to their duty, not out of indulgence, but due to submission to their general. He never yielded to them when they were insubordinate, but instead consistently resisted their demands. He disbanded the entire ninth legion with ignominy at Placentia, although Pompey was still in arms, and would not receive them again into his service until they had made repeated and humble entreaties and their ringleaders were punished.

When the soldiers of the tenth legion at Rome demanded their discharge and rewards for their service, with violent threats and no small danger to the city, despite the war raging in Africa, he met the legion and disbanded it. He addressed them as "Quirites" instead of "Soldiers." By using just one word, "Quirites," he completely won them over and altered their resolve. They immediately declared themselves his "soldiers" and followed him to Africa, despite having initially refused his service. He demonstrated great zeal and fidelity in serving his clients even as a young man.

When he had placed himself at the head of affairs, he advanced some of his most faithful adherents to the highest offices. He was criticized for this favoritism, and when questioned about it, he replied, "If I had been aided by robbers or cutthroats in defending my honor, I would have rewarded them equally." LXXIII Caius Memmius had published some extremely virulent speeches against him, but he responded with equal acrimony. Despite this, he later supported him with his vote and interest when he ran for consulship. Calvus, after publishing scandalous epigrams about him, attempted to reconcile with him through the mediation of friends. He then wrote to him, out of kindness, the first letter.

He had captured the pirates who had taken him, swearing to crucify them. He carried out his threat, but first ordered their throats to be cut. When summoned as a witness against Publicus Clodius, his wife Pompeia's gallant, who was prosecuted for profaning religious ceremonies, he declared he knew nothing of the affair. His mother Aurelia and sister Julia, however, provided the court with an exact and full account of the circumstances. He was asked why he had divorced his wife. "My family should be free from guilt, and even from suspicion." " LXXV

For while Pompey declared that he would consider those as enemies who did not take arms in defense of the republic, he desired it to be understood that he regarded those who remained neutral as his friends. On the field of Pharsalia, he called out to the soldiers, saying, "Spare your fellow citizens." Later, he granted permission to each soldier in his army to capture an enemy. Afranius and Faustus had taken up arms against him, after obtaining their pardon; and Lucius Caesar had not only in a cruel manner destroyed with fire and sword his freedmen and slaves, but also cut to pieces the wild beasts he had prepared for public entertainment.

He preferred to verify rather than punish anything that had been proposed or suggested. During his third and fourth consulships, he used only the title of the office, content with the power of dictator, which was conferred upon him along with the consulship. In both years, he substituted other consuls in his place for the last three months; thus, he held no assemblies of the people for the election of magistrates, except for tribunes and ediles, who were exempt. He also appointed officers under the name of praefects instead of praetors to manage city affairs during his absence.

Men should consider what is becoming when they speak with me, and view my words as a law. To such a pitch of arrogance did he proceed, that when a soothsayer announced to him an unfavourable omen, he said, "The entrails will be more favourable when I please; and it ought not to be regarded as a prodigy that a beast should be found wanting a heart." " LXXVIII This behaviour became increasingly intolerable in him because when Pontius Aquila, one of the tribunes of the people, refused to rise and greet him as he passed by the tribunes' seat during his triumph, he was so offended that he cried out, "Well then, you tribune, Aquila, will you not remove me from office?" And for some days afterwards, he never promised a favor to anyone, without this proviso: "If Pontius Pilate gives me leave." " LXXIX For when, after the sacred rites of the Latin festival, he was returning home, amidst the immoderate and unusual acclamations of the people, a man in the crowd placed a laurel crown on one of his statues. The tribunes, Epidius Marullus and Caesetius Flavus, then ordered the white fillet encircling the crown to be removed from the statue and the man who had done so to be taken into custody.

Caesar, concerned about the suggestion of royalty having little purpose, or fearing he would be denied the opportunity to refuse it, scolded the tribunes severely and removed them from office. He was never able to shake off the scandal of being associated with the king's name, despite responding to the people who hailed him as such. "I am Caesar," he said, "not a king." At the festival of Lupercalia, when Consul Antony placed a crown on his head at the rostra, he did so repeatedly, only to remove it each time. He then sent the crown to the Capitol for Jupiter. Instead of caballing any longer separately, in small parties, they now united their counsels. The people themselves were dissatisfied with the present state of affairs, both privately and publicly (49), condemning the tyranny under which they lived, and calling on patriots to assert their cause against the usurper.

Would you be now alive if fate had dealt differently? Because he drove from Rome, Brutus was first made consul. This man, because he put the consuls down, has been rewarded with a royal crown. After a public proclamation was issued for the senate to meet on the Ides of March, they accepted the proposed date and location, which suited their needs.

The Ides of March had arrived, and Julius Caesar, despite ominous signs, entered the Senate House with a sense of confidence, mocking Spurinna's warnings as mere superstition. He was stabbed with 23 wounds, uttering a groan only, but no cry, at the first wound. Although some authors relate that when Marcus Brutus fell upon him, he exclaimed, "What You are one of them. The whole assembly instantly dispersed. He lay for some time after he had expired, until three of his slaves laid the body on a litter and carried it home, with one arm hanging down over the side.

Two men, armed with swords and spears, suddenly set fire to the funeral bier using lighted torches. To the Father of His Country. His death was widely accepted as being in line with his own preferences.

For the first games, Augustus consecrated in honor of his memory, a comet blazed for seven days, rising at approximately eleven o'clock each day. It was believed to be the spirit of Caesar, now in heaven. As a result, he is depicted on his statue with a star on his brow. They died by drowning, while others were killed in combat, and a few took their own lives using the very dagger that had slain Caesar. The termination of the civil war between Caesar and Pompey marked a new epoch in Roman history. A republic that had endured for approximately 460 years, with unparalleled glory, eventually fell into a state of despotism from which it would never recover again. A sudden transition from prosperity to the ruin of public freedom, without the intervention of any foreign enemy, excites a reasonable conjecture that the constitution in which such a change could take place, however vigorous in appearance, must have lost its soundness of political health, enabling it to endure for so many ages.

This advantage was indeed diminished by the creation of Tribunes of the people. These individuals often embroiled the Republic in civil dissensions due to their ambition. Ultimately, they abused their authority to such an extent that they became instruments of aggrandizement for leading men in the state who could afford to purchase their favor. In general, however, the majority of the Tribunes who were actuated by views that comprehended the interests of the multitude rather than those of individuals, did not so much endanger liberty as they interrupted tranquillity in the public. When occasional commotions subsided, there was no permanent ground for establishing personal usurpation. The Romans became sensible as their state grew. In the year 312 of the City, two magistrates were first appointed to record the number of people and the value of their estates. Shortly thereafter, they were granted authority not only to inspect individuals' morals but also to impose public censure for any acts of licentiousness or indecency.

A prominent historian from that era states that venality was widespread among the Romans. A writer who emerged shortly thereafter notes that excessive luxury and decadence led to such overwhelming debt that even the prospect of civil war and chaos seemed almost tolerable. During his nine-year tenure of that province, he accumulated wealth on a scale so immense that it made him the wealthiest individual in the state. If nothing more than a splendid establishment had been the object of his pursuit, he would have attained to the summit of his wishes.

Caesar had many enemies in the Senate. His former friend Pompey remained cool towards him even after Julia's death. He purchased the friendship of Curio at the start of the civil war with a bribe worth nearly half a million pounds. It is to the honor of Caesar, that when he had obtained the supreme power, he exercised it with a degree of moderation beyond what was generally expected by those who had fought on the side of the Republic.
